explanations.5: The condition checks if the number of arguments is NOT equal to 2; the '!' means 'not' and '$#' counts how many arguments were passed.
explanations.13: The '&&' chains commands together—if the first part (checking if folder doesn't exist) is true, it runs the echo and exit commands.
explanations.16: This command saves your current directory location so you can return to it later with 'popd'; the '>/dev/null' hides the output message.
explanations.18: The backslash sequences like '\e[37;1m' are ANSI color codes that make the text appear bold and white on the terminal.
explanations.41: The '<(...)' syntax is called process substitution—it lets you treat the output of a command as if it were a file that 'diff' can read.
explanations.42: This extracts function names by finding lines starting with 'function', then using 'awk' to grab just the function name (the second field), and 'sort' to order them alphabetically.
